text:
When setting project goals, it is helpful to use the SMART framework. This means your goals should be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. For example, instead of saying “improve the website,” a SMART goal would be “increase website traffic by 20% within three months.”

Once you have your main goal, break it into smaller milestones. Each milestone represents an important step forward, like completing the design phase. You also need to define clear deliverables—tangible items your team produces, such as reports or prototypes.

Finally, ensure stakeholder alignment by discussing goals with everyone involved. This way, all team members and managers agree on the direction and expectations, which reduces confusion later.
